subtopic_detail: Cyclic mastalgia evaluation and first-line management
stem:
You are the GP registrar reviewing a 31-year-old para 1 woman with 6 months of bilateral breast heaviness, soreness and tenderness that begins around 7 to 10 days before each menstrual period and resolves with menses. She breastfed her child for 9 months and stopped 2 years ago. She drinks two coffees per day, exercises three times a week, and is not on hormonal contraception.

She is up to date with cervical screening and is not in a BreastScreen age group. Examination today, performed in the late luteal phase, reveals diffuse bilateral nodularity without a dominant mass, no skin changes, no nipple discharge and no axillary lymphadenopathy; her bra is supportive but not specifically fitted.

data_table.Examination: Diffuse bilateral nodularity with no dominant mass, no skin tethering or peau d'orange, no nipple discharge, no axillary lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: No imaging or bloods performed; menstrual cycle 28 days, regular

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange a bilateral diagnostic mammogram and a targeted ultrasound today and refer her urgently to a breast surgeon for a tissue diagnosis given the persistent nature of her bilateral breast symptoms over the past 6 months
options.rationale: Cyclic bilateral mastalgia without a dominant mass or other red flags has a very low malignancy yield from imaging; routine imaging is not the first step and urgent surgical referral is not indicated. (Source: RACGP: Mastalgia clinical guidance)

options.text: Commence a combined oral contraceptive pill containing 30 microgram ethinyloestradiol now as first-line therapy aimed at suppressing cyclical breast tissue stimulation and at reducing her cycle-related breast tenderness
options.rationale: Combined oral contraceptive pills can either improve or worsen mastalgia and are not recommended as first-line therapy for cyclic breast pain in a woman with regular cycles and no contraceptive need. (Source: RACGP: Mastalgia clinical guidance)

options.text: Recommend a strict caffeine-free diet, a low-fat diet under 15 percent of total energy and high-dose vitamin E 1000 IU daily as evidence-based interventions for cyclic mastalgia to use throughout her menstrual cycle each month
options.rationale: Evidence for caffeine restriction, low-fat diet and vitamin E in mastalgia is weak and inconsistent; these may be discussed but are not the recommended first-line intervention package. (Source: RACGP: Mastalgia clinical guidance)

options.text: Reassure her, advise a professionally fitted supportive bra including a sports bra at night during the symptomatic week and regular simple analgesia, and review at 3 months with a symptom diary linking pain to her cycle
options.rationale: Cyclic mastalgia without a dominant mass or red flags has reassurance, supportive bra fitting, analgesia and a 3-month diary as first-line management; imaging and pharmacotherapy are reserved for refractory cases. (Source: RACGP: Mastalgia clinical guidance)

options.text: Commence oral tamoxifen 10 mg daily for a 6-month course and refer her to a breast clinic for a discussion of danazol if the first-line treatment fails to provide adequate symptom control over the trial period
options.rationale: Tamoxifen and danazol are reserved for severe refractory cyclic mastalgia after lifestyle measures have failed; they carry significant adverse effects and are not first-line in primary care for an unscreened initial presentation. (Source: RACGP: Mastalgia clinical guidance)

explanation.summary: Cyclic mastalgia is common, bilateral and luteal-phase predominant. After excluding red flags (dominant mass, skin tethering, bloody nipple discharge, axillary nodes, age above BreastScreen threshold), first-line management is education, well-fitted supportive bra, simple analgesia and a symptom diary over 3 months. Imaging adds little in cyclic, bilateral, mass-free disease. Pharmacological options (tamoxifen, danazol) are reserved for severe refractory cases due to their adverse effect profile. Evidence for dietary interventions and vitamin E is weak.
key_points: Cyclic mastalgia: bilateral, luteal-phase, resolves with menses, often nodular
Red flags: dominant mass, skin tethering, blood-stained nipple discharge, axillary lymphadenopathy
First-line: supportive bra including sports bra at night during symptomatic week, simple analgesia, symptom diary
Tamoxifen 10 mg daily is reserved for severe refractory disease after lifestyle measures fail
explanation.pearls: A well-fitted supportive bra (even at night during the symptomatic week) achieves clinically meaningful relief in around 70% of women with cyclic mastalgia and should be the first prescription you write.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia: Breast cancer red flags

subtopic_detail: Vasa praevia recognition at second trimester scan
stem:
You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 33-year-old primigravid woman at 21+0 weeks gestation referred from a private radiology practice after her morphology scan. The transabdominal scan demonstrates a normally grown singleton fetus with normal anatomy, a posterior fundal placenta, and a velamentous cord insertion with two unprotected fetal vessels coursing within the membranes across the internal cervical os. Transvaginal imaging confirms type 1 vasa praevia (velamentous cord insertion) with the aberrant vessels within 1 cm of the internal os.

She is well, asymptomatic, with no bleeding, no contractions and a normal cervical length of 36 mm. Her IVF cycle used a single-embryo transfer and she had no first-trimester bleeding episodes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68, HR 78, SpO2 99% in air, T 36.6 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus, no bleeding, no contractions; speculum: closed cervix, no discharge or bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Morphology scan: singleton, posterior placenta, velamentous insertion, type 1 vasa praevia, cervical length 36 mm, normal Doppler

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Reassure her that vasa praevia diagnosed in the second trimester resolves spontaneously by term in around 80% of cases, plan a vaginal delivery at term with continuous CTG monitoring and discharge her from the high-risk obstetric clinic now
options.rationale: While placenta praevia frequently migrates, vasa praevia rarely resolves; vaginal delivery risks fatal fetal exsanguination from vessel rupture at membrane rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia statement)

options.text: Arrange an immediate cervical cerclage at 22 weeks gestation and bed rest for the remainder of the pregnancy followed by induction of labour at 39 weeks once fetal maturity has been clinically confirmed at term
options.rationale: Cerclage is not used in vasa praevia management and bed rest has no proven benefit; the established intervention is elective caesarean before onset of labour. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia statement)

options.text: Arrange a follow-up transvaginal ultrasound at 32 weeks, plan elective caesarean section between 34+0 and 36+0 weeks with antenatal corticosteroids beforehand, and counsel about inpatient admission from 30 to 32 weeks for monitoring
options.rationale: Diagnosed vasa praevia is managed with a follow-up scan in the third trimester, antenatal steroids in the late preterm period, planned elective caesarean section at 34 to 36 weeks before labour, and consideration of hospitalisation from around 30 to 32 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia statement)

options.text: Plan elective caesarean section at 39+0 weeks gestation with continuous CTG monitoring from 36 weeks and admission to the labour ward at 38 weeks for daily fetal surveillance with paper trace and ultrasound assessment
options.rationale: Delivery at 39 weeks risks spontaneous labour and membrane rupture leading to fetal exsanguination; planned caesarean between 34 and 36 weeks balances the risks of prematurity against the risk of catastrophic vessel rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia statement)

options.text: Offer termination of pregnancy at 22 weeks given the high perinatal mortality associated with undiagnosed vasa praevia and the diagnostic certainty achieved by the morphology ultrasound on the transabdominal and transvaginal scans
options.rationale: Prenatally diagnosed vasa praevia with planned caesarean section has excellent perinatal outcomes; termination is not an appropriate counselling offer based on this diagnosis alone. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia statement)

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia is the presence of unprotected fetal vessels in the membranes overlying or near the internal cervical os, typically associated with velamentous cord insertion (type 1) or vessels between placental lobes (type 2), and is most common after IVF and with low-lying or bilobed placentas. Undiagnosed vasa praevia carries fetal mortality above 50% at membrane rupture; prenatally diagnosed and managed with planned elective caesarean before labour, perinatal outcomes are excellent. The Australian package includes a follow-up scan, antenatal corticosteroids, hospitalisation around 30 to 32 weeks and elective caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks.
key_points: Risk factors: velamentous cord insertion, IVF pregnancy, bilobed/succenturiate placenta, low-lying placenta
Type 1: velamentous insertion with vessels across os; Type 2: vessels between placental lobes
Plan elective caesarean between 34+0 and 36+0 weeks, antenatal corticosteroids beforehand
Consider admission from 30 to 32 weeks given risk of unheralded membrane rupture
explanation.pearls: Prenatally diagnosed vasa praevia delivered by elective caesarean before labour has near-zero perinatal mortality; the only way to lose that benefit is to await spontaneous labour and risk vessel rupture at membrane rupture.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum thyroiditis recognition in transient hypothyroid phase
stem:
You are the GP registrar reviewing a 34-year-old para 1 woman 5 months postpartum who reports 6 weeks of progressive fatigue, intolerance of cold, weight gain of 3 kg above her usual postpartum weight despite reduced appetite, constipation, and a low mood that has not responded to a structured exercise plan. She had a euthymic pregnancy on no thyroid medications, an uncomplicated vaginal birth at 39+5 weeks, and is exclusively breastfeeding with no infant feeding concerns.

Her Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale today is 9 (cut-off 13). Examination is bland; she has mild non-tender diffuse goitre, no proptosis, no tremor, and her DTRs show a delayed relaxation phase bilaterally.

data_table.Examination: Mild diffuse non-tender goitre, no proptosis, no fine tremor, dry skin, delayed ankle reflex relaxation phase bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: TSH 14.2 mIU/L (RR 0.4-4.0), free T4 9.6 pmol/L (RR 10-22), anti-TPO antibodies positive at high titre, free T3 4.0 pmol/L (RR 3.5-6.5)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence oral propranolol 20 mg three times daily and arrange a thyroid uptake scan in 4 weeks to assess for subacute or postpartum thyroiditis in the thyrotoxic phase and to plan ongoing therapy from there
options.rationale: Her biochemistry shows hypothyroid (not thyrotoxic) phase disease, so a beta-blocker is not indicated and uptake imaging is not the next step in the hypothyroid phase of postpartum thyroiditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Postpartum thyroiditis)

options.text: Commence oral levothyroxine at 50 micrograms daily, recheck thyroid function in 6 to 8 weeks, plan to taper and trial cessation around 12 months postpartum, and counsel about lifelong hypothyroidism risk and recurrence
options.rationale: Symptomatic postpartum thyroiditis in the hypothyroid phase with TSH above 10 mIU/L and low free T4 is treated with levothyroxine; treatment is usually transient with attempted withdrawal at around 12 months, and lifelong hypothyroidism develops in roughly 20 to 30% of women. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Postpartum thyroiditis)

options.text: Refer her to a perinatal mental health service for an antidepressant trial of sertraline 50 mg daily for postnatal depression and recheck thyroid function only if she does not improve over a 4 to 6 week monitored treatment course
options.rationale: Her EPDS is below the screening cut-off and her clinical and biochemical picture clearly identifies postpartum thyroiditis as the explanation for her symptoms; antidepressant therapy is not the indicated next step. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Postpartum thyroiditis)

options.text: Refer her urgently to an endocrinologist for a radioactive iodine uptake scan to differentiate between postpartum thyroiditis and Graves disease before initiating any thyroid hormone replacement therapy in this woman
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine is contraindicated in breastfeeding and is not the diagnostic axis here; her hypothyroid biochemistry, anti-TPO positivity and timing are characteristic of postpartum thyroiditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Postpartum thyroiditis)

options.text: Repeat thyroid function tests in 6 weeks without treatment and counsel her that the changes are likely physiological postpartum and very likely to normalise spontaneously without need for any thyroid hormone replacement therapy
options.rationale: With a TSH above 10 mIU/L, a low free T4, anti-TPO positivity and clear hypothyroid symptoms, expectant management would prolong morbidity; treatment is indicated and improves symptoms within weeks. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Postpartum thyroiditis)

explanation.summary: Postpartum thyroiditis is an autoimmune destructive thyroiditis affecting 5 to 10% of postpartum women, usually within the first year. Three patterns: transient thyrotoxic only, transient hypothyroid only, or biphasic (thyrotoxic followed by hypothyroid). Symptomatic hypothyroidism with TSH above 10 mIU/L is treated with levothyroxine; treatment is typically transient with attempted withdrawal at around 12 months, although 20 to 30% develop permanent hypothyroidism. Beta-blockers are used in the thyrotoxic phase. Radioactive iodine uptake studies are contraindicated in breastfeeding women.
key_points: Postpartum thyroiditis: autoimmune destructive thyroiditis, 5 to 10% of postpartum women
Pattern: thyrotoxic only, hypothyroid only, or biphasic (thyrotoxic then hypothyroid)
Treat symptomatic hypothyroid phase with levothyroxine; attempt withdrawal at 12 months
20 to 30% develop permanent hypothyroidism so lifelong follow-up is required
explanation.pearls: Anti-TPO antibodies positive in early pregnancy predict postpartum thyroiditis: screen women with anti-TPO at 6 weeks, 3 and 6 months postpartum, and warn them that 'low mood and fatigue at 4 to 6 months' may be thyroid before it is depression.

subtopic_detail: Choice between vacuum and forceps for instrumental delivery
stem:
You are the obstetric registrar called to the birth suite for a 28-year-old primigravid woman at 39+4 weeks gestation in the second stage of labour. She has been actively pushing for 90 minutes after a 60-minute passive descent phase with adequate uterine contractions, an effective epidural and reassuring CTG. The presenting head is at +2 station, occiput-anterior position, with no caput or moulding, the bony skull is at the level of the ischial spines plus 2 cm and zero-fifths palpable abdominally.

The bladder is empty after a recent in-out catheter. Her membranes are ruptured with clear liquor. She and her partner are tiring and asking for help to complete the birth in a controlled and safe way.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78, HR 96, T 37.0 degrees C; CTG: baseline 140 bpm, normal variability, no decelerations
data_table.Examination: Cervix fully dilated, vertex OA, station +2, no caput or moulding, zero-fifths palpable abdominally, intact perineum
data_table.Investigations: Latest VBG: pH 7.34, BE -3.2, lactate 1.8 mmol/L (cord gas not yet)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate choice of instrument for an instrumental vaginal delivery in this setting?

options.text: Augment her labour with intravenous syntocinon at 12 mU/min for a further 60 minutes of active pushing before considering any instrumental or operative intervention, given her reassuring fetal heart trace
options.rationale: She has already had adequate contractions through 90 minutes of active pushing in a primigravid woman; further augmentation rather than expediting birth would be inappropriate at this point. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)

options.text: Proceed to a Kielland rotational forceps delivery to rapidly complete the second stage of labour, given her primiparity and the duration she has already spent actively pushing without effective progression of descent
options.rationale: Kielland forceps are reserved for malposition (occiput-transverse or occiput-posterior) requiring rotation; the fetus is OA so rotational forceps are not indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)

options.text: Proceed to Neville-Barnes outlet forceps delivery with infiltration of pudendal block local anaesthesia and a generous mediolateral episiotomy to minimise the risk of any obstetric anal sphincter injury during her birth
options.rationale: Outlet forceps would be reasonable when the head is on the perineum and visible without separating the labia; at +2 station vacuum is generally preferred for a non-rotational instrumental delivery in this OA position. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)

options.text: Proceed to a low-station vacuum-assisted delivery using a soft cup, applying axial traction with each contraction and aborting after three pulls without descent or three cup detachments per consensus criteria
options.rationale: Vacuum is preferred for a non-rotational outlet/low cavity delivery at OA position with a reassuring CTG, an empty bladder and minimal moulding/caput; the abort rule (three pulls or three detachments) is standard practice. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)

options.text: Proceed to category 2 caesarean section for failure to progress in the second stage of labour given the duration of active pushing and the maternal exhaustion now interfering with effective bearing-down efforts
options.rationale: She meets criteria for instrumental delivery (fully dilated, OA, low station, reassuring CTG, empty bladder, suitable operator); jumping to caesarean is not the most appropriate next step. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)

explanation.summary: For non-rotational low or outlet instrumental delivery with the fetus in occiput-anterior, vacuum is generally preferred over forceps because of lower maternal genital trauma and OASIS rates, although forceps have lower failure rates. Pre-requisites include full dilatation, ruptured membranes, known position, empty bladder, head no more than one-fifth palpable, station at or below the ischial spines, effective analgesia, suitable operator and theatre access for caesarean if unsuccessful. Kielland or other rotational forceps are reserved for malposition; abort the vacuum after three pulls without descent or three cup detachments.
key_points: Vacuum preferred for outlet or low non-rotational delivery in OA position
Pre-requisites: full dilatation, ruptured membranes, OA known position, empty bladder, head one-fifth or less palpable, station spines or below
Vacuum abort: three pulls without descent or three cup detachments
Rotational forceps (Kielland) only for malposition (OT or OP); requires experienced operator
explanation.pearls: If the head is at +2 station OA with reassuring trace and a tired primigravid, vacuum is the right tool; if there is malposition (OT or OP) or you need rapid delivery for non-reassuring trace with the head deeply engaged, forceps usually beats vacuum.

subtopic_detail: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy versus HELLP using Swansea criteria
stem:
You are the obstetric medicine registrar called to ED for a 32-year-old primigravid woman at 34+5 weeks gestation with 5 days of malaise, anorexia and right upper quadrant discomfort, now with 24 hours of polyuria and polydipsia, mild jaundice and confusion. She has had a normal pregnancy and her booking BP was 110/68. Today her BP is 138/86. She is hypoglycaemic at the bedside, oliguric and has marked coagulopathy with a prolonged INR without thrombocytopenia.

Liver biochemistry shows a moderate hepatocellular transaminase rise with significant hyperbilirubinaemia and a raised ammonia. Urate is markedly elevated. CTG is reassuring; ultrasound shows a bright liver with no biliary dilatation and the fetus is appropriately grown.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86, HR 110, T 37.0 degrees C, SpO2 98% in air, urine output 18 mL/hour
data_table.Examination: Drowsy with mild asterixis, scleral icterus, tender right upper quadrant, no peripheral oedema beyond mild ankle swelling
data_table.Investigations: BGL 2.4 mmol/L, ALT 312 U/L, AST 286 U/L, bilirubin 88 micromol/L, ALP 220 U/L, urate 0.52 mmol/L, ammonia 92 micromol/L, INR 2.1, platelets 168 x10^9/L, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, WCC 18.4 x10^9/L, creat 132 micromol/L, urine protein PCR 28 mg/mmol; liver ultrasound: bright echotexture, no dilated ducts

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence ursodeoxycholic acid 15 mg/kg/day and rifaximin 550 mg twice daily for presumed intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy and arrange induction of labour at 37+0 weeks in line with cholestasis-of-pregnancy guidance
options.rationale: Intrahepatic cholestasis presents with pruritus and raised bile acids without coagulopathy, hypoglycaemia or encephalopathy; delaying delivery to 37 weeks would risk maternal liver failure and fetal demise in AFLP. (Source: RANZCOG: Liver disease in pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange a percutaneous liver biopsy to differentiate acute fatty liver of pregnancy from autoimmune hepatitis or acute viral hepatitis before deciding whether to deliver and to guide subsequent immunosuppressive therapy choice
options.rationale: Liver biopsy is rarely indicated in pregnancy due to coagulopathy and time pressure; the diagnosis is established clinically and biochemically using Swansea criteria and definitive treatment is delivery, not awaiting histology. (Source: RANZCOG: Liver disease in pregnancy)

options.text: Stabilise with 10% dextrose infusion, correct coagulopathy with fresh frozen plasma and cryoprecipitate, give betamethasone if not already given, contact ICU and plan urgent delivery within 24 hours after maternal stabilisation
options.rationale: She fulfils at least 6 of the 14 Swansea criteria for acute fatty liver of pregnancy (vomiting, abdominal pain, polyuria/polydipsia, encephalopathy, raised bilirubin, hypoglycaemia, raised urate, raised transaminases, raised ammonia, coagulopathy, bright liver, leukocytosis); definitive treatment is prompt delivery after maternal stabilisation. (Source: RANZCOG: Liver disease in pregnancy)

options.text: Commence intravenous magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis and intravenous labetalol for tight blood pressure control, expect this to be HELLP syndrome, and continue conservative inpatient management to maximise the gestational age at delivery
options.rationale: HELLP requires thrombocytopenia (under 100 x10^9/L) which is absent here; the biochemistry pattern with hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy without thrombocytopenia, hyperammonaemia and bright liver fits acute fatty liver of pregnancy, and conservative management is not appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG: Liver disease in pregnancy)

options.text: Transfer her to a quaternary liver transplant centre for assessment of urgent transplantation as the primary therapy and recommend continuation of the pregnancy until transplant assessment is completed prior to any delivery
options.rationale: Liver transplantation is exceptional in AFLP and only considered for women who fail to improve after delivery; the immediate intervention is prompt delivery with intensive supportive care. (Source: RANZCOG: Liver disease in pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare third-trimester microvesicular steatosis with mortality if untreated. The Swansea criteria (need 6 of 14) include vomiting, abdominal pain, polyuria/polydipsia, encephalopathy, raised bilirubin (above 14 micromol/L), hypoglycaemia (under 4 mmol/L), raised urate (above 0.34 mmol/L), leukocytosis (above 11 x10^9/L), ascites or bright liver, raised transaminases (above 42 U/L), raised ammonia, renal impairment, coagulopathy, and microvesicular steatosis on biopsy. Discrimination from HELLP turns on coagulopathy without thrombocytopenia, hypoglycaemia, encephalopathy and hyperammonaemia. Treatment is maternal stabilisation, correction of coagulopathy, dextrose and prompt delivery.
key_points: Swansea criteria for AFLP: need 6 of 14; absence of microvesicular steatosis on biopsy does not exclude AFLP
HELLP vs AFLP: HELLP has thrombocytopenia and haemolysis; AFLP has hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy without thrombocytopenia, encephalopathy
Definitive therapy: maternal stabilisation then prompt delivery, usually within 24 hours
Recurrence risk in subsequent pregnancies up to 20 to 25%; LCHAD deficiency screening for the neonate
explanation.pearls: Hypoglycaemia in a jaundiced third-trimester woman is AFLP until proven otherwise; the combination of low BGL, coagulopathy without thrombocytopenia, and raised ammonia is the discriminator from HELLP and forces immediate delivery planning.

sources.label: BSG/RCOG: Liver disease in pregnancy guideline
